{{Short description|Men's association football team}}
{{About|the men's team|the women's team|Denmark women's national football team}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=March 2026}}

| Elo min date             = May 1967

| Captain                  = [[Pierre Emile Højbjerg]]

| rightarm2                = FFFFFF
| shorts2                  = FFFFFF
| socks2                   = FFFFFF
| First game               = {{fb|DEN}} 9–0 {{fbb-rt|FRA|1794|full=y}}<br />([[London]], [[England]]; 19 October 1908)
| Largest win              = {{fb|DEN}} 17–1 {{fb-rt|FRA|1794}}<br />([[London]], [[England]]; 22 October 1908)
| Largest loss             = {{fb|GER|Nazi}} [[Breslau Eleven|8–0]] {{fb-rt|DEN}}<br />([[Wrocław|Breslau]], [[Nazi Germany|Germany]]; 16 May 1937)
| World cup apps           = 6
| World cup first          = 1986
| World cup best           = Quarter-finals ([[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998]])
| Regional name            = [[UEFA European Championship|European Championship]]
| Regional cup apps        = 10
| Regional cup first       = [[1964 European Nations' Cup|1964]]
| Regional cup best        = '''Champions''' ([[UEFA Euro 1992|1992]])
| Confederations cup apps  = 1
| Confederations cup first = [[1995 FIFA Confederations Cup|1995]]
| Confederations cup best  = '''Champions''' ([[1995 FIFA Confederations Cup|1995]])
| 3rdRegional name         = [[CONMEBOL–UEFA Cup of Champions]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps     = 1
| 3rdRegional cup first    = [[Artemio Franchi Trophy#Editions##1993|1993]]
| 3rdRegional cup best     = Runners-up ([[Artemio Franchi Trophy#1993|1993]])
| medaltemplates           =
{{MedalSport|[[Association football|Men's football]]}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[UEFA European Championship]]}}
{{MedalGold|[[UEFA Euro 1992|1992 Sweden]]|[[UEFA Euro 1992 squads#Denmark|Team]]}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[FIFA Confederations Cup]]}}
{{MedalGold|[[1995 King Fahd Cup|1995 Saudi Arabia]]|[[1995 FIFA Confederations Cup squads#Denmark|Team]]}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Football at the Summer Olympics|Olympic Games]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1908 Summer Olympics|1908 London]]|[[Football at the 1908 Summer Olympics|Team]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1912 Summer Olympics|1912 Stockholm]]|[[Football at the 1912 Summer Olympics|Team]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1960 Summer Olympics|1960 Rome]]|[[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics|Team]]}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1948 Summer Olympics|1948 London]]|[[Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics|Team]]}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[Finalissima|CONMEBOL–UEFA Cup of Champions]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1993 Artemio Franchi Cup|1993 Mar del Plata]]|[[1993 Artemio Franchi Cup|Team]]}}
}}
The '''Denmark national football team''' ({{langx|da|Danmarks herre-fodboldlandshold}} or ''herrelandsholdet'') represents [[Denmark]] including [[Greenland]],<ref>{{cite web |year=2026 |title=Danmark i TV og Live Stream |trans-title=Denmark on TV and Live Stream |language=da |website=tvsporten.dk |url=https://www.tvsporten.dk/fodbold/danmark |access-date=2026-03-05 |quote={{lang |da |Det danske herrelandshold i fodbold repræsenterer Danmark, samt Grønland i alle internationale fodbold konkurrencer for herrer.}} ({{translation |The Danish men's national football team represents Denmark, including Greenland in all international football competitions for men.}}) }}</ref> but not [[Faroe Islands]], in men's international [[Association football|football]] competitions.<ref>{{cite news |last=Josevski |first=Aleksandar |date=2017-12-27 |title=DBU-formand: Grønland er et stykke fra optagelse i Fifa |trans-title=DBU chairman: Greenland is a long way from joining Fifa |language=da |work=[[DR (broadcaster)|Danish Broadcasting Corporation]] |url=https://www.dr.dk/sporten/fodbold/dbu-formand-groenland-er-et-stykke-fra-optagelse-i-fifa |access-date=2026-03-05 }}</ref>{{contradictory inline |article=2026 FIFA World Cup |section=Qualification |page=Talk:2026 FIFA World Cup#Why is Greenland, an autonomous territory of Denmark, depicted as "not a FIFA member"? |reason=The 2026 FIFA World Cup article shows Greenland (an autonomous territory of Denmark) as "not a FIFA member" in (1) the map in section "Qualification" and (2) the map in section "Voting". In contrast, this article says "the Denmark national football team [...] represents Denmark and Greenland in men's international football competitions." |date=February 2026}} It is controlled by the [[Danish Football Association]] (DBU), the governing body for the football clubs which are organised under DBU. Denmark's home stadium is [[Parken Stadium]] in the [[Østerbro]] district of [[Copenhagen]].

Denmark were winners of the unofficial [[Football at the 1906 Intercalated Games|1906 Intercalated Games]] football competition and silver medalists at the [[Football at the 1908 Summer Olympics|1908]] and [[Football at the 1912 Summer Olympics|1912 Olympics]]. However, as amateurs who prohibited their internationals from becoming professionals at foreign clubs, Denmark did not qualify for the [[FIFA World Cup]] until [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986]], although they won another Olympic silver in [[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics|1960]].

Since the 1980s and the "[[#Danish Dynamite (1980–1990)|Danish Dynamite]]" team, Denmark has remained competitive in international tournaments. Triumph in the [[UEFA Euro 1992|1992 European Championship]] in Sweden marked the team's most prominent victory, defeating defending champions [[Netherlands national football team|the Netherlands]] in the semi-final and world champions [[Germany national football team|Germany]] in the final. They also won the [[1995 King Fahd Cup]], defeating [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] in the final. Their best World Cup result was achieved in 1998, where they narrowly lost 3–2 in a quarter-final against [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]]. Denmark also made the round of 16 in 1986, 2002 and 2018. Their most prominent result in recent history was at [[UEFA Euro 2020|Euro 2020]], where the team lost in the semi-final against [[England national football team|England]] after extra time.

==History==

===Amateur years===
[[File:DK football1908.jpg|thumb|left|200px|The Danish team that won their first silver medal at the [[1908 Summer Olympics]]. [[Harald Bohr]] is in the top row, 2nd from left.]]
[[File:Football at the 1912 Summer Olympics - Denmark squad.JPG|thumb|right|200px|Danish team, winning the silver medals at the [[1912 Summer Olympics|1912 Olympics]]]]
On 12 April 1896, Denmark XI defeated Podilatikos Syllogos Athinon, either 9–0 or 15–0, at the [[Neo Phaliron Velodrome]] in [[Athens]] in a demonstration game during the 1896 Olympic Games.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tableso/ol1896f.html |title=Games of the I. Olympiad |language=English |date=3 February 2022 |website=[[RSSSF]] |accessdate=4 October 2021}}</ref>

On 18 April 1897, a game was played in [[Hamburg]] when a selection team from the [[Danish Football Association]] defeated a selection team from the Hamburg-Altona Football Association, 5–0 in the presence of 5,000 spectators.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.dbu.dk/om-dbu/dbu-s-historie/1889-1920/ |title=1889-1920 i dansk fodbold |trans-title=1889-1920 in Danish football |website=www.dbu.dk |language=Danish |accessdate=26 November 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.tz.de/sport/fussball/daenische-nationalmannschaft-spieler-trainer-erfolge-rekorde-laenderspielbilanz-or-90404104.html |title=Dänische Nationalmannschaft |publisher=TTZ |language=de |date=3 May 2021 |accessdate=4 May 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.totalbold.dk/fodboldens-indtog-i-danmark-1889-til-1908/ |title=Fodboldens indtog i Danmark: 1889 til 1908 |trans-title=The entry of football into Denmark: 1889 to 1908 |language=Danish |publisher=Danish Ballgame Union |date=26 December 2008 |accessdate=4 May 2021}}</ref>

The first three editions of the Olympic football event in 1900–1906 had an unofficial status, as the event was not yet open for national football teams to compete, and only had limited participation of three or four club teams from a few nations. Denmark had no club team invited in the [[Football at the 1900 Summer Olympics|1900 Olympics]] and the [[Football at the 1904 Summer Olympics|1904 Olympics]], but then received a special invitation for the unofficial [[Football at the 1906 Summer Olympics|1906 Olympics]], to compete against one Greek club team (Athens) and two club teams from the Ottoman Empire ([[İzmir|Smyrna]] and [[Thessaloniki]]). The team to represent Denmark was compiled of players from the [[Copenhagen Football Association]] (KBU), and they won the event, and thereby an unofficial gold medal. Two years later, in the first official football tournament at the [[Football at the 1908 Summer Olympics|1908 Olympics]], Denmark won a silver medal. At the next Olympics, in [[Football at the 1912 Summer Olympics|1912]], the team again won a silver medal, which was followed by a golden era from July 1912 until August 1920, with Denmark ranked most of the time as number one in the world by the [[World Football Elo Ratings|Elo ranking]]. The first official national football match was played on 19 October 1908 during the [[1908 Summer Olympics|Olympic Games]] in [[London]]. Denmark beat France's second team 9–0 in the tournament's quarter-finals.

Although Denmark figured fairly prominently in the pre-[[FIFA World Cup]] era, international success would elude them for years from the first World Cup in 1930 and forward. Despite the country's ability to produce outstanding football talents, the [[Danish Football Union]] (DBU) only had the ambition (or economy) to send the national team to play friendly matches and in the regional tournament, the [[Nordic Football Championship|Nordic Championship]], from October 1920 until June 1948. When DBU opted to set their sights higher, they allowed the national team to start contesting the Olympics again, promptly resulting in a bronze medal at the [[Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics|1948 Olympics]]. After, the team only reached the quarter-final at the [[Football at the 1952 Summer Olympics|1952 Olympics]], with the DBU choosing not to contest the next [[Association football at the 1956 Summer Olympics|1956 Olympics]]. As football remained an amateur past-time, most of the best Danish footballers moved abroad to make a living,<ref name="history">{{cite web|title=Danish Football|url=http://www.ambtallinn.um.dk/NR/rdonlyres/5D568E99-F599-440C-8B01-0057FC717638/0/fodbold_engelsk_2003.pdf|author=Birger B. Peitersen|publisher=Royal Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs in collaboration with Danmark's Nationalleksikon|year=2003|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110612040833/http://www.ambtallinn.um.dk/NR/rdonlyres/5D568E99-F599-440C-8B01-0057FC717638/0/fodbold_engelsk_2003.pdf|archive-date=12 June 2011}}</ref> and due to DBU enforcing the rule to bar all professionals from the national team, it started to become difficult to assemble a highly competitive team.

Denmark experienced their next revival at the [[Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics|1960 Olympics]] with a third set of Olympic silver medals. This was followed by another notable performance at the [[1964 European Nations' Cup]], where Denmark impressively finished in fourth place. However, this finish was considered by many as being more the result of a comparatively easy draw rather than a result of a well-playing team. In order for Denmark to qualify for the semi-final, they only had to defeat [[Malta national football team|Malta]], [[Albania national football team|Albania]] and [[Luxembourg national football team|Luxembourg]]. In the semi-final, Denmark fell 3–0 to the [[Soviet Union national football team|Soviet Union]], then lost the third-place match to [[Hungary national football team|Hungary]].

The strict rule of only allowing amateurism at the national team was finally abolished by the DBU in May 1971, as they had acknowledged this change was needed in order to build a highly competitive team. In February 1978, when the DBU also decided to allow professional football to be introduced in the Danish leagues, the way was at the same time paved for the national team to sign its first sponsorship with the well-known Danish brewery [[Carlsberg Group|Carlsberg]]. The new sponsorship enabled the DBU to hire the German [[Sepp Piontek]] in July 1979 as the first full-time professional coach of the national team. The full transition of the national team from amateurism to professionalism had now been accomplished, and indeed, this would soon lead to a vast improvement in the performances of the team.<ref name="history"/>

According to Rob Smyth and Lars Eriksen, authors of a book on the "Danish Dynamite" team that would soon emerge:
{{cquote|That process [the transition to professionalism] was accelerated by the fact that so many of the national team were playing abroad, and values learned there were slowly seeping in. ... Denmark got a headstart on football globalisation, benefiting from the enlightenment and experience that comes with playing abroad. At [[UEFA Euro 1984|Euro 84]], their 20-man squad contained 14 overseas-based players; the other seven teams had only five between them. At [[1986 FIFA World Cup|Mexico 86]], their squad included players from the champions of [[Serie A|Italy]], [[Bundesliga|West Germany]], [[Football League First Division|England]], [[Eredivisie|Holland]] and [[Belgian First Division A|Belgium]], but not [[Danish 1st Division|Denmark]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/blog/2009/oct/13/forgotten-story-denmark-1980s |title=The forgotten story of ... Danish Dynamite, the Denmark side of the mid-80s |first1=Rob |last1=Smyth |first2=Lars |last2=Eriksen |work=[[guardian.co.uk]] |date=13 October 2009 |access-date=10 June 2012 |location=London}}</ref>}}

===Danish Dynamite (1980–1990)===
In the [[1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|1982 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Denmark finished with four wins and four losses, including a 3–1 win against the eventual [[1982 FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] champions [[Italy national football team|Italy]], but Denmark failed to qualify for the final tournament. Qualification for [[UEFA Euro 1984]] saw Denmark defeat [[England national football team|England]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1924)|Wembley Stadium]] when [[Allan Simonsen]] converted a [[Penalty kick (association football)|penalty kick]] for a 1–0 win. Denmark qualified for their first international tournament since 1964, and the team was dubbed "Danish Dynamite" in a competition for the official Danish Euro 1984 song.<ref name="history" /> Denmark's participation ended in the semi-final when the team lost on [[Penalty shootout (football)|penalties]] to [[Spain men's national football team|Spain]], most remembered for [[Preben Elkjær]]'s penalty miss, his shorts torn apart. Following the strong performance at the finals, the name "Danish Dynamite" became a mainstay for the following decade of the Denmark national team under head coach Sepp Piontek.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/blog/2009/oct/13/forgotten-story-denmark-1980s | location=London | work=The Guardian | first1=Rob | last1=Smyth | first2=Lars | last2=Eriksen | title=The forgotten story of ... Danish Dynamite, the Denmark side of the mid-80s | date=13 October 2009}}</ref>

Denmark made their first World Cup appearance in the [[1986 FIFA World Cup|1986 World Cup]], and with the attacking duo of [[Michael Laudrup]] and Preben Elkjær, thrashed [[Uruguay national football team|Uruguay]] 6–1. In the second round, Denmark once again faced Spain and once more lost, 5–1, including four goals by [[Emilio Butragueño]]. The first Spanish goal was caused by a miss-timed backpass by [[Jesper Olsen]] to Butragueño, an unfortunate action subsequently coined as "a real Jesper Olsen" ("''en rigtig Jesper Olsen''"). The phrase would live on for 13 years, and was repeated by the Danish TV commentators in 1999, when an identical backpass was carried out by [[Jesper Grønkjær]] to [[Filippo Inzaghi]] in Grønkjær's debut for the national team.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Den aften, da Jesper Olsen blev glemt |journal=Politiken.dk|date=28 March 1999|language=da}}</ref>

Denmark qualified for [[UEFA Euro 1988|Euro 1988]], and nearly made the [[Football at the 1988 Summer Olympics|1988 Olympics]]. [[Richard Møller Nielsen]] guided Denmark to a secured spot for the final tournament&nbsp;– ahead of West Germany, but following the discovery that Dane [[Per Frimann]] was not eligible for the team's 2–0 win over [[Poland national football team|Poland]], Denmark was penalised, and subsequently failed to qualify. At Euro 1988, Denmark's participation ended in early defeat after Denmark lost all the group games to Spain 3–2, West Germany 2–0 and Italy 2–0. They then failed to qualify for the [[1990 FIFA World Cup|1990 World Cup]]; Sepp Piontek resigned as head coach of the national team in April 1990, where he was replaced by Richard Møller Nielsen.

===The Møller Nielsen fairytale: 1992 European Championship===
Denmark began Euro 1992 qualification with a home victory against the [[Faroe Islands national football team|Faroe Islands]], and followed up with an away draw against [[Northern Ireland national football team|Northern Ireland]] and a 2–0 home loss against [[Yugoslavia national football team|Yugoslavia]]. Brothers [[Michael Laudrup]] and [[Brian Laudrup]] opted to quit the national team in November 1990. When Nielsen subsequently decided to dismiss quality players such as [[Jan Mølby]] and [[Jan Heintze]] from the squad due to disciplinary problems, several newspapers began demanding that Nielsen step down as head coach. Despite this, Denmark won the rest of their five matches in the qualification group, including a 2–1 away win against Yugoslavia. This was not enough to qualify, as the team still had to settle for second place in the group, behind Yugoslavia.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20170828040850/http://www.uefa.com/uefaeuro/season=1992/standings/round=218/group=75/index.html 1992 Qualification group - UEFA]</ref>

Due to international sanctions resulting from the [[Yugoslav wars]], [[UEFA]] announced on 31 May 1992 – only 10 days before the competition – that [[Yugoslavia]] was excluded from the competition and their place given to Denmark, who finished as the second-placed team in its qualifying group.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Yugoslavia barred from European Championships |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1992/05/31/Yugoslavia-barred-from-European-Championships/3160707284800/ |access-date=31 March 2022|website=UPI}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Sports ban hits Yugoslavia - UPI Archives |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1992/06/01/Sports-ban-hits-Yugoslavia/9026707371200/ |access-date=7 January 2024|website=UPI}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Yugoslavia banned for 1994 World - UPI Archives |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1992/10/01/Yugoslavia-banned-for-1994-World/8232717912000/ |access-date=7 January 2024|website=UPI}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Lowe |first=Sid|date=29 May 2020|title=Slavisa Jokanovic: 'Euro 92 was taken away from us. We were better than Denmark' |url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2020/may/29/slavisa-jokanovic-euro-92-taken-away-better-than-denmark |access-date=7 January 2024|newspaper=The Guardian |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>

Contrary to popular belief,<ref name="history" /> the team did not enter the tournament completely unprepared, after having rallied home from seaside vacations. The majority of players had been assembled to play a friendly match against the [[CIS national football team|CIS]] when Denmark officially received Yugoslavia's spot.<ref>{{cite book|title=Schmeichel: The Autobiography|first=Peter|last=Schmeichel|pages=97–98 |isbn=0-7535-0444-8 |year=1999|publisher=Virgin }}</ref> Relying heavily on goalkeeper [[Peter Schmeichel]] and his defense, as well as creative spark Brian Laudrup – having decided to make a comeback for the national team in April 1992 – the Danish team created one of the biggest surprises in the event's history, as they went on to win the [[UEFA European Championship|European Championship]] trophy under head coach Nielsen's defensive playing style. Advancing from the group stage ahead of England and [[France national football team|France]], Denmark defeated the Netherlands – the defending Euro 1988 champions – on penalties in the semi-final. Later, in the [[UEFA Euro 1992 Final|final]], Denmark won 2–0 win against reigning [[1990 FIFA World Cup|World Cup]] champions Germany, ensuring Denmark its first international trophy.

===Decline and revival (1993–2000)===
In August 1993, Michael Laudrup decided to settle his ongoing dispute with Richard Møller Nielsen about the team tactics and made a comeback to the national team. However, the following years Denmark saw mixed results, as they first failed to qualify for the [[1994 FIFA World Cup|1994 World Cup]], but then won the [[1995 King Fahd Cup|1995 Confederations Cup]] (then known as King Fahd Cup), beating [[Copa América]] champions [[Argentina national football team|Argentina]] 2–0 in the final. As defending champions at [[UEFA Euro 1996|Euro 1996]], Denmark disappointed with a lackluster performance after an early elimination in the group stage. The team had achieved a respectable win against [[Turkey national football team|Turkey]], a draw against [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]] and only a defeat to [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]].

Nielsen's replacement was Swede [[Bo Johansson|Bo "Bosse" Johansson]], who gave the team a more offensive strategy, and the [[1998 FIFA World Cup|1998 World Cup]] saw the revival of the Danish team, starring both Laudrup brothers in their last international campaign. After defeating [[Saudi Arabia national football team|Saudi Arabia]] 1–0, drawing with [[South Africa national football team|South Africa]] and losing 2–1 to eventual champions France, Denmark defeated [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]] 4–1 in the round of 16. They exited in the quarterfinals with a 3–2 loss to [[Brazil national football team|Brazil]].

Without the Laudrup brothers, Denmark qualified for Euro 2000, only to lose all three matches in the group stage.

===The Olsen Gang (2000–2015)===
[[File:FIFA World Ranking Evolution for Denmark national football team.svg|thumb|400px|[[FIFA World Rankings]] for Denmark, August 1993&nbsp;– July 2009]]
[[Morten Olsen]] was named the new manager for Denmark in 2000 and the team was quickly dubbed the "Olsen Gang", a reference to [[Olsen-banden|the series of Danish movies]] based around the main character [[Egon Olsen]] and his genius (criminal) plans. The nickname was also used for the Danish team as an alternative to the more commonly used "Danish Dynamite", already in those days when Olsen played as a captain. Denmark's tactics shifted from the preferred 4–4–2 formation practised by Bo Johansson to an even more attacking style with an emphasis on the wingers available at the time, namely [[Jesper Grønkjær]] and [[Dennis Rommedahl]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Olsen looking for another shock |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/euro_2004/denmark/players_to_watch/3520770.stm|author=BBC Sport|date=24 May 2004|work=BBC News}}</ref> Accordingly, Olsen's preferred formation was either a 4–3–3 or a 4–2–3–1.<ref>{{cite web|title=Morten Olsen ist Europas Fußball-Großvater|url=http://www.nordschleswiger.dk/SEEEMS/20000.asp?artid=26351|author=Nordschleswiger.dk|date=13 October 2010|language=de|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110719130349/http://www.nordschleswiger.dk/SEEEMS/20000.asp?artid=26351|archive-date=19 July 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Danmark er forvandlet mod Island|url=http://politiken.dk/sport/fodbold/EM-kvalifikation/ECE1053875/danmark-er-forvandlet-mod-island/|author=Politiken.dk|date=7 September 2010|language=da}}</ref> Olsen even possessed an outspoken opposition to the 4–4–2 system, as he threatened to leave his position as head coach in the event he was asked to deploy Denmark in that formation.<ref>{{cite web|title=Olsen: 4–4–2? Ikke med mig!|url=http://www.bt.dk/sport/olsen-4-4-2-ikke-med-mig|author=BT.dk|date=19 November 2007|language=da}}</ref> To support the development of players for the new tactical 4–3–3 system, all national youth teams also changed their formation.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sådan spiller de danske landshold|url=http://www.dbu.dk/news/newsShow.aspx?id=238884|author=DBU|date=16 May 2007|language=da}}</ref> Another change Olsen brought to the national team was to stress the importance of only using fit players who had been granted regular playing time at their club. However, at times he was forced to compromise from this principle, as the pool of players available in the relatively small nation did not always provide him viable substitute options.

Denmark qualified both for the [[2002 FIFA World Cup|2002 World Cup]] and [[UEFA Euro 2004|Euro 2004]], but despite impressive results in the group stage in both tournaments, especially the 2–0 win against reigning World Cup winners France in 2002, Denmark failed to advance any further. At the 2002 World Cup, Denmark was defeated by England in the round of 16, and at Euro 2004, they were eliminated in the quarter-finals against the [[Czech Republic national football team|Czech Republic]].

For the [[2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)#Group 2|2006 World Cup qualification]], Denmark was paired with 2002 semi-finalists Turkey and eventual Euro 2004 champions [[Greece national football team|Greece]]. Following a poor start to qualification, Denmark was trailing both Turkey and [[Ukraine national football team|Ukraine]]. After failing to qualify for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]], Morten Olsen considered leaving the job, having received several offers from club teams, but decided to stay and extended his contract until after the [[2010 FIFA World Cup|2010 World Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Morten Olsen forlænger med fire år|url=http://www.dr.dk/Sporten/Fodbold/Landsholdet/2005/11/21/151254.htm|author=DR Sporten|date=21 November 2005|language=da}}</ref>

Denmark failed to qualify for [[UEFA Euro 2008|Euro 2008]], but qualified for the 2010 World Cup by winning their qualifying group, earning two wins against Sweden and four points against Portugal. Olsen's contract was extended for two more years, until [[UEFA Euro 2012|Euro 2012]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Morten Olsen fortsætter til 2012 |url=http://www.dbu.dk/Nyheder/2010/Januar/morten_olsen_fortsaetter_til_2012.aspx|author=DBU|date=8 January 2010|language=da}}</ref> At the 2010 World Cup, Denmark was grouped with [[Japan national football team|Japan]], [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]] and the Netherlands. Denmark lost the first match 2–0 to the Netherlands, but then scored a 2–1 victory against Cameroon. The game against Japan, however ended with a 3–1 defeat.

In qualification for Euro 2012, Denmark was once again grouped with Portugal; Denmark secured first place in the group, qualifying directly for the final tournament. Out of eight qualifying matches, Denmark won six, drew one and lost one, resulting Olsen's contract to be extended until after the [[2014 FIFA World Cup|2014 World Cup]].

At Euro 2012, Denmark were drawn in the proclaimed "[[group of death]]", [[UEFA Euro 2012 Group B|Group B]], alongside Germany, the Netherlands and Portugal. After defeating the Netherlands 1–0, they lost 3–2 to Portugal, requiring a win against Germany to advance in the tournament. Despite [[Michael Krohn-Dehli]]'s equaliser, Denmark lost 2–1 to Germany, and with Portugal defeating the Netherlands 2–1, Denmark was eliminated from the tournament after finishing third in Group B.

Denmark failed to qualify for the next two tournaments under Olsen. They finished second in their 2014 World Cup qualifying group but failed to achieve a play-off berth as they were the worst UEFA group runners-up after removing results against the last-place teams.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 October 2013 |title=Group B: Denmark miss out despite finishing second |url=https://www.eurosport.com/football/world-cup/2014/group-b-denmark-miss-out-on-playoff-despite-finishing-second_sto3965207/story.shtml |access-date=2 March 2024 |website=Eurosport |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Denmark beat Malta 6-0 but fail to qualify for World Cup |url=https://www.daijiworld.com/news/newsDisplay?newsID=195367 |access-date=2 March 2024 |website=www.daijiworld.com |language=en}}</ref> In [[UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying|Euro 2016 qualifying]] they finished third in their group, behind [[Albania national football team|Albania]] and eventual winners Portugal, but lost to Sweden in the play-offs.

When measuring the performance of the national team by winning share and earned points, Olsen's reign of the Denmark national team from July 2000 until January 2012 had at the time been the second-most successful in the era of full-time professional coaches, which began in July 1979. As of January 2012, Olsen had a winning share of 52.8%, and with three points for a victory and one for a draw, an average of 1.84 points per match.<ref name="MO games">{{cite web|title=Morten Olsen coach profile|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCoachInfo.aspx?coachid=4118&teamtypeid=330&genderid=1|author=DBU|language=da|access-date=21 September 2010|archive-date=17 June 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110617031052/http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCoachInfo.aspx?coachid=4118&teamtypeid=330&genderid=1|url-status=dead}}</ref> In comparison, Richard Møller Nielsen still had the best record among the professional coaches of the national team, with a 54.8% winning share and an average of 1.89 points per game.<ref name="RMN games">{{cite web|title=Richard Møller Nielsen coach profile|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCoachInfo.aspx?coachid=4116|author=DBU|language=da|access-date=21 September 2010|archive-date=23 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923230459/http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCoachInfo.aspx?coachid=4116|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===Hareide era: 2016–2020===
New coach [[Åge Hareide]] presided over the Danish campaign during the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|qualification]] stages.<ref name="Guardian">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2015/dec/10/denmark-hire-former-malmo-manager-age-hareide|title=Denmark hire former Malmo manager Age Hareide on two-year contract|newspaper=The Guardian|date=10 December 2015|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> Denmark, ranked number 24 in the [[FIFA World Rankings]] at the time of the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)#Seeding|draw]], were drawn into [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group E|Group E]], alongside [[Poland national football team|Poland]], [[Montenegro national football team|Montenegro]], [[Romania national football team|Romania]], [[Armenia national football team|Armenia]], and [[Kazakhstan national football team|Kazakhstan]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.uefa.com/worldcup/news/newsid=2267048.html|title=European teams learn World Cup qualifying fate|publisher=[[UEFA]]|date=25 July 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150905025044/http://www.uefa.com/worldcup/news/newsid%3D2267048.html|archive-date=5 September 2015}}</ref>
Despite suffering early defeats to Poland and Montenegro,<ref name="ESPN">{{cite web|url=http://www.espn.com/soccer/match?gameId=431840|title=Poland vs Denmark|publisher=ESPN|date=8 October 2016|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref><ref name="Guardian2">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2016/oct/11/fatos-beciraj-montenegro-stun-denmark-world-cup-roundup|title=Montenegro stun Denmark while Robert Lewandowski grabs Poland's winner|newspaper=The Guardian|date=11 October 2016|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> the Danes rallied, and secured second place in the group, and a berth in the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round|play-offs]], with a 1–0 away to Montenegro.<ref name="Reuters1">{{cite news|url=https://uk.reuters.com/article/uk-soccer-worldcup-mne-dnk/denmark-edge-montenegro-to-keep-alive-world-cup-hopes-idUKKBN1CA2OK|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180904230016/https://uk.reuters.com/article/uk-soccer-worldcup-mne-dnk/denmark-edge-montenegro-to-keep-alive-world-cup-hopes-idUKKBN1CA2OK|url-status=dead|archive-date=4 September 2018|title=Denmark edge Montenegro to keep alive World Cup hopes|work=Reuters|date=10 October 2017|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> Denmark were rewarded for their second-place finish with a play-off against [[Republic of Ireland national football team|the Republic of Ireland]].<ref name="UEFA1">{{cite web|url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/news/newsid=2509320.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171018060425/http://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/news/newsid=2509320.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=18 October 2017|title=European Qualifiers: World Cup play-off draw|publisher=UEFA|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> Denmark secured qualification with a 5–1 win at the [[Aviva Stadium]] in [[Dublin]], which included a [[hat-trick]] by [[Christian Eriksen]], who added to his tally of eight goals in qualification.<ref name="RTÉ">{{cite web|url=https://www.rte.ie/sport/soccer/2017/1114/920088-ireland-match-report/|title=Eriksen-inspired Denmark demolish Irish World Cup dreams|author=Leahy, Ed|website=RTÉ|date=15 November 2017|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref>

Denmark were [[2018 FIFA World Cup seeding|allocated]] into Pot 3 in advance of the [[2018 FIFA World Cup#Draw|draw]] for the [[2018 FIFA World Cup]].<ref name="FIFA2">{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/news/y=2017/m=9/news=oc-for-fifa-competitions-approves-procedures-for-the-final-draw-of-the-2907924.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170914215739/http://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/news/y=2017/m=9/news=oc-for-fifa-competitions-approves-procedures-for-the-final-draw-of-the-2907924.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=14 September 2017|title=OC for FIFA Competitions approves procedures for the Final Draw of the 2018 FIFA World Cup|publisher=FIFA|date=14 September 2017|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> They were drawn into [[2018 FIFA World Cup Group C|Group C]], alongside [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]], [[Peru national football team|Peru]], and [[France national football team|France]].<ref name="FIFA3">{{cite web|url=https://resources.fifa.com/mm/document/tournament/competition/02/66/71/62/2018fwc_matchschedule_20122017_en_neutral.pdf|title=Match Schedule|publisher=FIFA|access-date=4 September 2018|archive-date=24 June 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180624041527/https://resources.fifa.com/mm/document/tournament/competition/02/66/71/62/2018fwc_matchschedule_20122017_en_neutral.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> Their campaign in Russia began in the [[Mordovia Arena]], [[Saransk]], against Peru. A [[Yussuf Poulsen]] goal in the second half secured three points for the Danes.<ref name="Peru">{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/06/16/sports/world-cup/denmark-peru.html|title=Denmark Spoils Peru's World Cup Return|author=Mather, Victor|newspaper=New York Times|date=16 June 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> However, they struggled against Australia in their second tie, as Christian Eriksen's early goal was cancelled out by a [[Video Assistant Referee|VAR]]-awarded penalty, converted by [[Mile Jedinak]], in a game that ended 1–1.<ref name="Jedinak">{{cite web|url=https://www.thelocal.dk/20180621/denmark-frustrated-by-australia-in-world-cup-draw|title=Denmark frustrated by Australia in World Cup draw|publisher=The Local dk|date=21 June 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> Denmark secured qualification in their final group game, playing out the only goalless draw in the entire tournament,<ref>{{cite news |author=Tass |title=Record number of games without 0–0 draws at 2018 World Cup |url=https://www.upi.com/Record-number-of-games-without-0-0-draws-at-2018-World-Cup/7541529775097/ |publisher=United Press International |date=23 June 2018 |access-date=22 July 2018 }}</ref>
in the [[Luzhniki Stadium]] against France; a result which saw both teams progress.<ref name="0-0">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/jun/26/denmark-france-world-cup-group-c-match-report|title=Denmark join France in last 16 after first goalless draw of World Cup|author=James, Stuart|newspaper=The Guardian|date=26 June 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref>

Denmark faced [[Croatia national football team|Croatia]] in the [[2018 FIFA World Cup knockout stage#Croatia vs Denmark|round of 16]], with the Croats topping [[2018 FIFA World Cup Group D|Group D]] with three wins.<ref name="Irish Times">{{cite news|url=https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/soccer/international/ivan-perisic-strikes-for-croatia-to-end-iceland-s-world-cup-journey-1.3544912|title=Ivan Perisic strikes for Croatia to end Iceland's World Cup journey|author=Hytner, David|newspaper=The Irish Times|date=26 June 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> [[Mathias Jørgensen]] scored within the first minute of the game to give Denmark the lead, but [[Mario Mandzukic]] equalised only three minutes later, leaving the teams equally perched at one goal apiece.<ref name="Jorgenson">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/jul/01/croatia-denmark-world-cup-last-16-match-report|title=Croatia send Denmark out of World Cup after Subasic heroics in shootout|newspaper=The Guardian|date=1 July 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> The match remained at 1–1 after ninety minutes, necessitating thirty minutes of [[extra-time]], which still failed to separate the teams, as [[Luka Modrić]] saw his late penalty saved by goalkeeper [[Kasper Schmeichel]].<ref name="Jorgenson"/> As such, the tie was to be decided with a [[penalty shoot-out (association football)|penalty shoot-out]], with Schmeichel and his Croatian counterpart, [[Danijel Subašić]], positioning themselves on the line to face five penalties each.<ref name="Beeb">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/44591346|title=Croatia 1–1 Denmark|author=Fletcher, Paul|publisher=BBC|date=1 July 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> Croatia advanced, as Subašić saved three shots from Eriksen, [[Lasse Schöne]] and [[Nicolai Jørgensen]] respectively. [[Ivan Rakitić]] scored the decisive penalty to eliminate the Danes,<ref name="Beeb"/> putting an end to their best World Cup campaign since 2002.<ref name="2002!">{{cite news|url=https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/soccer/international/group-c-christian-eriksen-can-carry-denmark-into-last-16-1.3505501?mode=sample&auth-failed=1&pw-origin=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.irishtimes.com%2Fsport%2Fsoccer%2Finternational%2Fgroup-c-christian-eriksen-can-carry-denmark-into-last-16-1.3505501|title=Group C: Christian Eriksen can carry Denmark into last-16|newspaper=The Irish Times|author=Madden, Patrick|date=27 May 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref>

Following the World Cup, Denmark prepared for participation in the inaugural iteration of the [[UEFA Nations League]], having been drawn in [[2018–19 UEFA Nations League B|League B]] against [[Wales national football team|Wales]] and the Republic of Ireland.<ref name="Nations">{{cite web|url=https://www.uefa.com/uefanationsleague/news/0253-0d81efc3a33f-dd2083a5720d-1000--uefa-nations-league-2018-19-league-phase-draw/|title=UEFA Nations League 2018/19 League Phase draw|publisher=UEFA|date=24 January 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> The tournament offers an alternative route of qualification for the [[UEFA European Championships|European Championship]] and World Cup,<ref name="RTÉ2">{{cite web|url=https://www.rte.ie/sport/soccer/2018/0904/991521-uefa-nations-league-how-it-all-works/|title=UEFA Nations League – how it all works|author=Leahy, Ed|website=RTÉ|date=4 September 2018}}</ref> and increases the number of competitive games for international sides, replacing [[Exhibition game|friendlies]].<ref name="Telegraph">{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/football/teams/england/10722834/England-ready-to-play-in-new-Nations-League-as-revolutionary-Uefa-plan-earns-unanimous-backing.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220111/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/football/teams/england/10722834/England-ready-to-play-in-new-Nations-League-as-revolutionary-Uefa-plan-earns-unanimous-backing.html |archive-date=11 January 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=England ready to play in new Nations League as revolutionary UEFA plan earns unanimous backing|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=25 March 2014|author=Rumsby, Ben|access-date=4 September 2018}}{{cbignore}}</ref>

{{main|2018 Slovakia v Denmark football match}}
Owing to a dispute with the [[Spillerforeningen|players' union]] regarding the commercial rights of the players,<ref name="JohnJensen">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/sep/04/denmark-futsal-players-squad-contract-negotiatons-dbu-football|title=John Jensen takes charge of Denmark as row over players' rights goes on|newspaper=The Guardian|date=4 September 2018|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> the [[Danish Football Union]] named an entirely uncapped squad, consisting of a mixture of [[futsal]] players and players from the [[Danish 2nd Division]] and the [[Denmark Series]] (the third and fourth tier of Danish football respectively), in lieu of the regular side, to face [[Slovakia national football team|Slovakia]] in a pre-Nations League friendly, and it was feared that the same squad would be used for the competitive fixture against Wales.<ref name="NationsLeagueWD">{{cite news |title=Nations League: Denmark set to field 'futsal' players against Wales due to dispute |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/45382474 |work=[[BBC Sport]] |access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref><ref name="Beeb2">{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/45382474|title=Nations League: Denmark name futsal players in squad for Wales game amid dispute|publisher=BBC|date=4 September 2018}}</ref> The dispute arose due to the [[image rights]] of the players,<ref name="TheLocaldk2">{{cite web|url=https://www.thelocal.dk/20180904/could-denmark-cancel-international-matches-due-to-player-contract-row|title=Could Denmark cancel international matches over player contract row?|publisher=The Local dk|date=4 September 2018|access-date=5 September 2018}}</ref> with [[Hummel International|Hummel]] having the exclusive right to the manufacture and marketing of Danish kits.<ref name="HummelTime">{{cite web|url=https://www.soccerbible.com/performance/football-apparel/2016/08/hummel-release-special-edition-all-white-denmark-kit/|title=Hummel Release Special Edition All White Denmark Kit|publisher=SoccerBible|date=31 August 2016|access-date=5 September 2018}}</ref> The [[Danish FA|DBU]] wanted to use individual players, without explicit permissions, in their advertising.<ref name="TheIrishTimes3">{{cite news|url=https://www.irishtimes.com/sport/soccer/international/denmark-to-field-futsal-players-against-wales-due-to-dispute-1.3617938|title=Denmark to field futsal players against Wales due to dispute|newspaper=The Irish Times|date=4 September 2018|access-date=5 September 2018}}</ref><ref name="Telegraph2">{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/football/2018/09/04/futsal-players-amateurs-ex-arsenal-midfielder-temporary-charge/ |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220111/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/football/2018/09/04/futsal-players-amateurs-ex-arsenal-midfielder-temporary-charge/ |archive-date=11 January 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Futsal players, amateurs and an ex-Arsenal midfielder in temporary charge – a bitter contract dispute has decimated Denmark's team to face Wales|author=Wilson, Jeremy|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=4 September 2018}}{{cbignore}}</ref> Hareide would similarly not be involved, with the side instead falling under the temporary management of [[John Jensen]], with Hasse Kuhn serving as the assistant manager.<ref name="JohnJensen"/> The Danish FA was under a four-year [[probation]]ary period with [[UEFA]] for having forfeited a [[2019 FIFA Women's World Cup qualification|Women's World Cup qualification]] [[2019 FIFA Women's World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 4|game]] against [[Sweden women's national football team|Sweden]] in 2017 due to a similar dispute with the [[Denmark women's national football team|women's team]],<ref name="Deutsche">{{cite web|url=https://www.dw.com/en/denmark-cancels-womens-world-cup-qualifier-over-pay-dispute/a-41011980|title=Denmark cancels Women's World Cup qualifier over pay dispute|publisher=Deutsche Welle|date=18 October 2017|access-date=4 September 2018}}</ref> and a further violation could have resulted in Denmark being prohibited from participation in either the [[2018–19 UEFA Nations League]] or the [[UEFA Euro 2020|2020 European Championship]].<ref name="GuardianOp">{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/sep/04/the-fiver-denmark-football-something-rotten|title=Something is rotten in the state of Danish football|author=Glendenning, Barry|newspaper=The Guardian|date=4 September 2018 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231013223948/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/sep/04/the-fiver-denmark-football-something-rotten |archive-date= 13 October 2023 }}</ref> After a temporary agreement enabled the return of the regular players, the Danes won their first Nations League fixture 2–0.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Denmark's Christian Eriksen fires double to give Wales reality check |first=James |last=Candy |work=The Guardian |date=9 September 2018 |access-date=10 September 2018 |url= https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/sep/09/denmark-wales-nations-league-match-report |url-status=live |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20180910034408/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2018/sep/09/denmark-wales-nations-league-match-report|archive-date= 10 September 2018 }}</ref>

Hareide managed Denmark during [[UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying]] and won four matches, drawing four more. This meant Denmark qualified for the tournament, but due to the [[COVID-19 pandemic]] the tournament was postponed to 2021, and Hareide's contract expired on 30 June 2020. He was replaced by [[Kasper Hjulmand]].

===Hjulmand era: 2020–2024===
At [[UEFA Euro 2020]], Denmark were beaten 1–0 by [[Finland national football team|Finland]]; in a match marked by [[Christian Eriksen]]'s heart attack in the 42nd minute<ref>{{cite web |access-date=12 June 2021 |date=12 June 2021 |title=Christian Eriksen victime d'un grave malaise pendant Danemark - Finlande |url=https://www.lequipe.fr/Football/Actualites/Christian-eriksen-victime-d-un-grave-malaise-pendant-danemark-finlande/1261747 |website=[[L'Équipe]]}}<!-- auto-translated from French by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> which led to the match being suspended for over two hours. In the following match, Denmark lost their second match to [[Belgium national football team|Belgium]] 2–1, despite initially taking the lead. They avoided elimination in the final round of matches with an impressive 4–1 victory against [[Russia national football team|Russia]]. They therefore became the first team to qualify for the knockout phase of a European Championship despite two opening defeats. Facing [[Wales national football team|Wales]] in the round of 16, Denmark won by a large margin (4–0) which earned them a quarter-final tie with the [[Czech Republic national football team|Czech Republic]]. Denmark won the tie 2–1 to progress to the next round.<ref name="europe1">{{cite web |access-date=9 July 2021 |author=Julien Ricotta |date=3 July 2021 |title=Euro : ce qu'il faut retenir de la belle victoire danoise face à la République tchèque |url=https://www.europe1.fr/sport/euro-ce-quil-faut-retenir-de-la-belle-victoire-danoise-face-a-la-republique-tcheque-4055852 |website=[[Europe 1]]}}<!-- auto-translated from French by Module:CS1 translator --></ref> Their European Championship run ended with an extra time defeat to [[England national football team|England]] in the semi-finals.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.skysports.com/football/news/11095/12351311/england-2-1-denmark-kasper-hjulmand-bitter-over-raheem-sterling-penalty-call-in-euro-2020-semi-final |title=England 2-1 Denmark: Kasper Hjulmand bitter over Raheem Sterling penalty call in Euro 2020 semi-final |website=Sky Sports |date=8 July 2021 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231013162533/https://www.skysports.com/football/news/11095/12351311/england-2-1-denmark-kasper-hjulmand-bitter-over-raheem-sterling-penalty-call-in-euro-2020-semi-final |archive-date= 13 October 2023 }}</ref> [[Mikkel Damsgaard]] opened the scoring with a sublime free-kick on the half-hour mark before England equalised and subsequently won in extra time. Despite the elimination, Denmark's performance was widely praised by the sporting press, in particular for its resilience in overcoming the difficult situation caused by Eriksen's heart condition in the first group match, and for its high-quality teamwork and attacking play.<ref name="europe1"/>

In the [[2022–23 UEFA Nations League A]], they finished second in their group, only one point behind Croatia and winning twice against world champions [[France national football team|France]].<ref>{{Cite news |title=Cornelius scores twice as Denmark stun France |language=en-GB |work=BBC Sport |url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/football/61684539 |date=3 June 2022 |access-date=17 December 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |author=Will Magee |url=https://www.eurosport.com/football/uefa-nations-league/2022-2023/denmark-v-france-nations-league-live_sto9156688/story.shtml |title=Denmark 2-0 France: Danes ease to win over France, finish second in Nations League group |website=Eurosport |date=25 September 2022 }}</ref> Denmark also qualified for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup]], topping [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group F|Group F]] in the qualification campaign ahead of [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]], with nine victories, eight of which without conceding a goal.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Denmark qualify for 2022 World Cup |url=https://www.goal.com/en/news/denmark-qualify-for-2022-world-cup/1wzv8ncwlak6v1mot5gu1yquje |date=12 October 2021 |access-date=17 December 2022 |website=Goal.com }}</ref><ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Gastelum |first=Andrew |date=12 October 2021 |title=Denmark Punches Ticket to 2022 World Cup in Qatar |url=https://www.si.com/soccer/2021/10/12/denmark-qualifies-2022-world-cup-qatar-eriksen-maehle |access-date=28 December 2023 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Hjulmand: Denmark happy to be World Cup dark horses |url=https://www.fifa.com/news/origin1904-p.cxm.fifa.com/hjulmand-denmark-happy-to-be-world-cup-dark-horses |access-date=28 December 2023 |website=www.fifa.com |language=en}}</ref>

At the 2022 World Cup, Denmark were drawn again with France, [[Tunisia national football team|Tunisia]] and [[Australia men's national soccer team|Australia]] in [[2022 FIFA World Cup Group D|group D]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=25 November 2022 |title=How France, Denmark, Tunisia, Australia can qualify for knockouts: FIFA World Cup, Group D points table, scenarios |url=https://sportstar.thehindu.com/football/fifa-world-cup/news/fifa-world-cup-qatar-2022-group-d-round-of-16-qualification-scenario-points-table-game-schedule-france-denmark-tunisia-australia/article66182611.ece |access-date=17 December 2022 |website=Sportstar |language=en}}</ref> They began their campaign with a goalless draw with Tunisia.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Denmark 0-0 Tunisia: Danes held to stalemate as Christian Eriksen makes major tournament return |url=https://www.skysports.com/football/news/12098/12753002/denmark-0-0-tunisia-danes-held-to-stalemate-as-christian-eriksen-makes-major-tournament-return |access-date=17 December 2022 |website=Sky Sports |language=en}}</ref> In the second game, Denmark lost 2–1 to France after a brace from [[Kylian Mbappé]].<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Creditor |first=Avi |title=Mbappe Dazzles vs. Denmark; France Clinches World Cup Last-16 Spot |url=https://www.si.com/soccer/2022/11/26/france-denmark-world-cup-2022-video-highlights-recap |access-date=17 December 2022 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |date=26 November 2022 |language=en-us}}</ref> With a win essential, Denmark were unable to defeat Australia and were therefore eliminated; finishing bottom of their group.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ronald |first=Issy |date=30 November 2022 |title=Australia stuns Denmark to reach World Cup knockout stages |url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/11/30/football/denmark-australia-world-cup-2022-spt-intl/index.html |access-date=17 December 2022 |website=CNN |language=en}}</ref>

The [[UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group H|UEFA Euro 2024 qualifiers]] were also a tough affair for Denmark, seeded first in the draw but struggling in most of their matches, including a humiliating 3–2 defeat in [[Kazakhstan national football team|Kazakhstan]] after leading 2–0. Nevertheless, the Danish team managed to finish top of their group with 7 wins, 1 draw and 2 defeats, and qualified for their 10th continental finals.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Delaney books Denmark's Euro 2024 berth in 2-1 win over Slovenia |url=https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/delaney-books-denmarks-euro-2024-berth-2-1-win-over-slovenia-2023-11-17/ |access-date=30 June 2024 |website=[[Reuters]] |date=17 November 2023 }}</ref> At [[UEFA Euro 2024]], Denmark qualified as runners-up in their group but were eliminated by [[Germany national football team|Germany]] in the round of 16.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Douglas |first=Steve |date=29 June 2024 |title=Germany beats Denmark 2-0 to advance to Euro 2024 quarterfinals after storm stops play |url=https://apnews.com/article/germany-denmark-euro-2024-b9d346ab4cb92b2d6e829091de65258b |access-date=30 June 2024 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref> Following the tournament, Hjulmand resigned on 19 July 2024, and his assistant [[Morten Wieghorst]] was named as the new manager on a short-term contract until the end of 2024.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kasper Hjulmand stopper som landstræner |url=https://dbu.dk/nyheder/2024/juli/kasper-hjulmand-stopper-som-landstraener/ |access-date=19 July 2024 |website=[[Danish Football Association]] |date=19 July 2024 }}</ref>

===Riemer era: 2024–present===
Morten Wieghorst never managed the team in a competitive game, since he had to go on sick leave on 26 August 2024, and he was replaced as caretaker by [[Lars Knudsen (football manager)|Lars Knudsen]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Morten Wieghorst sygemeldt med stress-symptomer|language=da|date=26 August 2024|access-date=24 October 2024|publisher=dr.dk|url=https://www.dr.dk/nyheder/indland/morten-wieghorst-sygemeldt-landsholdsudtagelse-aflyst}}</ref> Knudsen oversaw Denmark's first four matches of the [[2024–25 UEFA Nations League A|UEFA Nations League]], beginning with a 2–0 win against [[Switzerland national football team|Switzerland]] on 5 September 2024.

On 24 October 2024, former [[RSC Anderlecht|Anderlecht]] manager [[Brian Riemer]] was named new manager of the Danish national football team.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://dbu.dk/nyheder/2024/oktober/brian-riemer-er-ny-landstraener-for-herrelandsholdet/|title=Brian Riemer er ny landstræner for Herrelandsholdet (Brian Riemer new manager of the Danish national team)|publisher=Danish FA|date=24 October 2024|access-date=24 October 2024|language=da}}</ref> His first match in charge of Denmark saw them fall to a 2–1 loss against [[Spain national football team|Spain]] during the UEFA Nations League group stage.

On 23 March 2025, Denmark played against [[Portugal national football team|Portugal]] during the [[2024–25 UEFA Nations League A|Nations League quarter-finals]],<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fpf.pt/pt/News/Todas-as-not%C3%ADcias/Not%C3%ADcia/news/48826/contextid/172 |title=Internacionais portugueses distinguidos |publisher=Portuguese Football Federation |language=pt |date=23 March 2025 }}</ref> which ended in a 5–3 aggregate loss for Denmark after extra time; this was the first time that Denmark had qualified for the knockout stage of the [[UEFA Nations League]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.uefa.com/uefanationsleague/match/2043062/ |title=Portugal 5–2 Denmark |publisher=UEFA |date=23 March 2025 }}</ref>

In the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]], Denmark finished second in [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group C|Group C]] behind [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]] following a 4–2 defeat in [[Glasgow]].<ref>{{cite news |title=UEFA's World Cup 26 qualifying in stats |url=https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/uefas-world-cup-26-qualifying-in-stats |access-date=3 April 2026 |work=[[FIFA]] |date=19 November 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251207194714/https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/uefas-world-cup-26-qualifying-in-stats |archive-date=7 December 2025}}</ref> A 2–2 home draw with [[Belarus national football team|Belarus]] in November 2025, when Denmark could have taken a decisive step towards automatic qualification, was widely criticised in the Danish media and later cited as one of the key setbacks of the campaign.<ref name="ReutersWC">{{cite news |last1=Lund |first1=Tommy |title=Danish soul-searching begins as Czechs end World Cup dreams |url=https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/denmark-enter-period-transition-after-missing-2026-world-cup-2026-04-02/ |access-date=3 April 2026 |work=[[Reuters]] |date=3 April 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Murray |first1=Ewan |title=Denmark hit by sickness bug before crunch World Cup qualifier with Scotland |url=https://www.theguardian.com/football/2025/nov/16/denmark-hit-by-sickness-bug-before-crunch-world-cup-qualifier-with-scotland |access-date=3 April 2026 |work=The Guardian |date=16 November 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251218170524/https://www.theguardian.com/football/2025/nov/16/denmark-hit-by-sickness-bug-before-crunch-world-cup-qualifier-with-scotland |archive-date=18 December 2025}}</ref> Denmark then advanced to the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round|European play-offs]], beating [[North Macedonia national football team|North Macedonia]] 4–0 in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round#Path D|Path D]] semi-final before losing the final to the [[Czech Republic national football team|Czech Republic]] on penalties after a 2–2 draw following extra time, thereby failing to qualify for the World Cup. The qualification failure marked Denmark’s first absence from a major tournament since [[UEFA Euro 2016|Euro 2016]].<ref>{{cite news |title=World Cup 2026 European play-off semi-finals highlights, round-up: Italy, Sweden, Türkiye earn final places |url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/news/02a3-203dbf45b100-5ecc63ed1293-1000--world-cup-2026-european-play-off-semi-finals-highlights-ro/ |access-date=3 April 2026 |work=[[UEFA]] |date=26 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Czechia 2–2 Denmark highlights (3–1 on pens): Czechia book 2026 World Cup spot with play-off final win |url=https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/news/02a3-2047bcf476d8-369543fe9786-1000--czechia-2-2-denmark-3-1-pens-2026-world-cup-play-off-final/ |access-date=3 April 2026 |work=UEFA |date=31 March 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260403111505/https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/news/02a3-2047bcf476d8-369543fe9786-1000--czechia-2-2-denmark-3-1-pens-2026-world-cup-play-off-final/ |archive-date=3 April 2026}}</ref> In the aftermath of the defeat, Riemer came under increased pressure in Danish football coverage, though the [[Danish Football Association]] (DBU) publicly backed him to continue.<ref name="ReutersWC" />

==Team image==

===Supporters===
[[File:Soccer crowd Copenhagen.jpg|thumb|Crowd watching the Denmark–Sweden Euro 2004 match at the [[City Hall Square, Copenhagen|Copenhagen City Hall Square]]]]
Apart from the national team, Denmark is famous for its traveling fans, known as ''roligans''. The movement emerged during the 1980s as the declared opposition to [[football hooliganism|hooliganism]]. The goal of the roligan movement is calm, yet cheerful, support during the matches, as "''rolig''" means "calm" in the [[Danish language]]. The ''roligans'' have since developed an image of easy-going nature and rabid support, and are often considered amongst the world's best national team fans, along with the [[Tartan Army]] of Scotland. They were collectively given the FIFA FairPlay Award at the 1984 European Championships.<ref>{{cite web|title=Historie -De Danske Roligans|url=http://www.roliganklubben.dk/hvem-er-vi/historie.html|author=RoliganKlubben|language=da|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090728212220/http://www.roliganklubben.dk/hvem-er-vi/historie.html |archive-date=28 July 2009}}</ref> The term "roligan" was invented by the editors of the Danish newspaper ''[[B.T. (tabloid)|B.T.]]'' in 1985, and later included in the Danish dictionary.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Mie Kirstine Rasmussen |date=1 July 2015 |title=I dag siger vi farvel til roliganen |language=Danish |publisher=[[DR (broadcaster)|DR]] |url=http://www.dr.dk/sporten/fodbold/i-dag-siger-vi-farvel-til-roliganen |accessdate=27 April 2016}}</ref> Just before the 1986 World Cup, the roligan movement was organized in order to support the national team at the tournament.

{{main articles|Denmark v Sweden (UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying)}}
The reputation of the Danish supporters was sullied by an attack from a fan which occurred in June 2007 in the [[Denmark v Sweden (UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying)|Euro 2008 qualifying match against Sweden]], when an enraged Danish supporter invaded the pitch and tried to attack the referee, following his decision to hand out a red card for Christian Poulsen and a penalty kick to Sweden in the last minute of the match. The attacking fan was stopped by some of the Danish football players on the field before he reached the referee, but due to the episode, the match was immediately cancelled by the referee, with UEFA subsequently deciding to award a 3–0 default defeat to Denmark as punishment.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://sport.tv2.dk/fodbold/2007-06-08-sverige-vinder-30-over-danmark|publisher=[[TV2 Danmark]]|title=Sverige vinder 3-0 over Danmark|date=8 June 2007|access-date=6 June 2026|language=da}}</ref>

===Rivalry===
{{Main|Denmark–Sweden football rivalry}}
Denmark's main rival is [[Sweden men's national football team|Sweden]]. The countries have played against each other 107 times, of which Denmark have won 40, drawn 20 and lost 47. The first match between the teams was an 8–0 Denmark win in May 1913. Denmark won their first five matches against Sweden before their first defeat in October 1916, 4–0.

The first competitive match between the countries was a 1–0 loss for Denmark in the group stage of [[UEFA Euro 1992]]. Both teams advanced from the group stage and Denmark went on to win the tournament. In [[UEFA Euro 2004]] the teams drew 2–2 in the last group match, ensuring that both teams advanced at the expense of [[Italy national football team|Italy]]. In Denmark's home match against Sweden in the [[UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying|qualification]] for [[UEFA Euro 2008]], the visitors [[Denmark v Sweden (UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying)|were awarded a 3–0 win]] after a Danish fan invaded the pitch and attacked the referee. The reverse fixture ended in a goalless draw and Denmark failed to qualify for the final tournament.

In the [[2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)|qualification]] for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]], Denmark won both matches against Sweden 1–0 and qualified for the World Cup. In the play-off round of the [[UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying|qualification]] for [[UEFA Euro 2016]], Denmark lost to Sweden 4–3 on aggregate and failed to qualify for the finals. The most recent match between the countries was a 2–1 win for Denmark in June 2024.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sweden men's national football team: record v Denmark |url=https://www.11v11.com/teams/sweden/tab/opposingTeams/opposition/Denmark/|website=11v11.com |access-date=9 September 2018}}</ref>

===Kit===
{{Commons|Denmark national football team kits}}
The team kit is produced by Danish company [[Hummel International|Hummel]]. It is all-red with white details to reflect the colours of the [[Flag of Denmark|Danish national flag]]. Prior to the period of [[Adidas]] sponsorship, the kit provider was also Hummel from 1979 to 2004.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Herrelandsholdets sponsor|url=https://www.dbu.dk/landshold/herrelandshold/a-landsholdet/sponsorer|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181009131913/https://www.dbu.dk/landshold/herrelandshold/a-landsholdet/sponsorer|archive-date=9 October 2018|website=DBU|language=da}}</ref>
{| class="wikitable"
|-
!Kit provider
!Period
|-
|{{flagicon|DEN}} [[Hummel International|Hummel]]
|1979–2004
|-
|{{flagicon|GER}} [[Adidas]]
|2004–2016
|-
|{{flagicon|DEN}} [[Hummel International|Hummel]]
|2016–present
|-
|}

===Broadcast===
All of Denmark's qualification, [[UEFA Nations League|Nations League]] and friendly matches are broadcast by [[TV 2 (Danish TV channel)|TV 2]] on television. [[DR (broadcaster)|DR]] holds the rights on radio transmission.

==Home stadium==
{{For|stadium history|Parken Stadium}}
[[File:Kparken.JPG|thumb|245px|right|[[Parken Stadium]], with a capacity of 38,065 spectators, located in Copenhagen, and used as official home stadium by the Denmark football team]]
The first home match for the Denmark national team was a 2–1 friendly win over England on 25 May 1910, taking place with 12,000 spectators at the stadium "KBs baner" (located in the area "Granen", where the [[Forum Copenhagen]] later was built).<ref>{{cite web|title=Landskampsstatistik -KBs baner (København)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCountryInfo.aspx?stadiumid=1113&teamtypeid=330&genderid=1#match|author=DBU|language=da|access-date=27 October 2010|archive-date=13 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180113204416/https://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCountryInfo.aspx?stadiumid=1113&teamtypeid=330&genderid=1#match|url-status=dead}}</ref> As the facilities were considered too small, a new stadium in [[Copenhagen]] was built with the name '''[[Københavns Idrætspark|Idrætsparken]]'''. Since its opening on 25 May 1911, it has hosted all the home matches of the football club [[Kjøbenhavns Boldklub|KB]] and most of the home matches of the national team. During the first 82 years of the national team, from October 1908 until November 1990, Denmark played a total number of 254 A-level home matches, of which 232 were played in Idrætsparken. Among all the played home matches in Idrætsparken, Denmark achieved 125 victories (54%), 41 draws (18%) and 66 defeats (28%). The stadium reached a record of 51,600 spectators for the 1–2 game against Sweden in June 1957.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landskampsstatistik -Idrætsparken (København)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCountryInfo.aspx?stadiumid=1124#stat|author=DBU|language=da|access-date=21 September 2010|archive-date=13 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180113204023/https://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCountryInfo.aspx?stadiumid=1124#stat|url-status=dead}}</ref>

In regards of the first era from 1908 to 1990, only 22 home matches were played at other stadiums than Idrætsparken, of which 11 took place at [[Aarhus Idrætspark]] (then known as Atletion) in [[Aarhus]], followed by [[Aalborg Stadion]] in [[Aalborg]] with six games, and the remaining five matches hosted by four other stadiums. Whenever another stadium than Idrætsparken was used for a home match, it was only for some of the less important friendly games or Olympic qualifiers, while only Idrætsparken was used to host the matches related to bigger tournaments, like the [[Nordic Football Championship|Nordic championship]], the European Championship and World Cup. When a major rebuild of Idrætsparken began in December 1990, the subsequent two official Euro 1992 qualification matches were moved to [[Fionia Park|Odense Stadium]] in [[Odense]].

On 9 September 1992, the rebuilt Idrætsparken, now called '''[[Parken Stadium|Parken]]''' and later renamed '''[[Telia Company|Telia]] Parken''' for sponsorship reasons, became the biggest venue in Denmark with a capacity of 42,358 spectators, and was opened with a friendly 1–2 defeat against Germany. This first match in Parken was viewed by 40,500 spectators,<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Tyskland (1–2)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=1518|author=DBU|date=9 September 1992|language=da}}</ref> while the current stadium record of 42,099 spectators was set on 8 October 2005 for a World Cup qualifying match against Greece.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Grækenland (1–0)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=4635|author=DBU|date=8 October 2005|language=da}}</ref>

The capacity of the stadium was later reduced to 38,009 seats after the upper part of the D-side was converted from 4,000 extra seats into office and VIP areas in June 2009.<ref>{{cite web|title=Sædekategorier i Parken|url=http://www.dbu.dk/sitecore/content/Faelles/landshold/Billetsalg/saedekategorier_i_parken.aspx|author=DBU|date=13 May 2010|language=da|access-date=25 October 2010|archive-date=23 April 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110423183751/http://www.dbu.dk/sitecore/content/Faelles/landshold/Billetsalg/saedekategorier_i_parken.aspx|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Superbest tribune står klar medio 2009|url=http://www.fck.dk/nyhedsvisning/?newsid=7383|author=FCK official website|date=29 October 2007|language=da|access-date=25 October 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101109184538/http://fck.dk/nyhedsvisning/?newsid=7383|archive-date=9 November 2010|url-status=dead}}</ref> Despite this decrease in capacity, the stadium is today still the [[List of football stadiums in Denmark|biggest venue in Denmark]] by far; the next largest venue, [[Brøndby Stadium]], only has a capacity of 26,000 seats. From September 1992 to May 2006, Parken was the sole stadium of all the men's senior national team matches. However, meagre spectator support at some of the friendly matches at Parken, which from 2000 to 2005 attracted an acceptable average of 23,862 per match – but down to 9,598 for the friendly 3–1 win over [[Israel national football team|Israel]] in April 2002<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Israel (3–1)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=3924|author=DBU|date=17 April 2002|language=da}}</ref> – prompted the DBU to start hosting many of the friendly matches to other stadiums around Denmark. These other stadiums had less spectator capacity than Parken, but just about enough to cover the Danes' somewhat lesser interest for friendly matches.

On 27 May 2006, Parken's 13-year monopoly on national team matches was broken for the first time when Denmark played a friendly match at the Aarhus Idrætspark against upcoming 2006 World Cup participants [[Paraguay national football team|Paraguay]]. Tickets to this match were sold out quickly, with almost 19,000 of the 20,227 tickets sold within the first hour of sale,<ref>{{cite web|title=Billetter til Århus-landskamp revet væk |url=http://www.dr.dk/Sporten/Fodbold/Landsholdet/2006/02/24/121539.htm|author=DR Sporten|date=24 February 2006|language=da}}</ref> and was support-wise a big success with a total audience of 20,047 spectators.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Paraguay (1–1)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=4967|author=DBU|date=27 May 2006|language=da}}</ref> When the two next friendly matches were played at other new venues, such as a match on 16 August at Fionia Park in Odense against [[Poland national football team|Poland]], and on 1 September at Brøndby Stadium against Portugal, the number of spectators dropped. The Poland match attracted only 11,088 spectators,<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Polen (2–0)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=5030|author=DBU|date=16 August 2006|language=da|access-date=25 October 2010|archive-date=13 December 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141213053101/http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/Landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=5030|url-status=dead}}</ref> while the Portugal match had only 13,186 fans.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Portugal (4–2)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=5033|author=DBU|date=1 September 2006|language=da|access-date=25 October 2010|archive-date=15 October 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141015205714/http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=5033|url-status=dead}}</ref> In particular, the Portugal match was deemed as a support-wise failure because it featured a higher-ranked opponent, which spectators normally would have a high interest to watch. The main reasons why Brøndby Stadium failed to attract a high number of spectators were generally thought to be a combination of the poor rainy weather and the fact the stadium is located on the outskirts of Copenhagen, and as such does not attract a new demographic audience compared to Parken, due to the travel distances being more or less the same for the majority of the population.

Currently, while most of the friendly matches continue to be played at some of the smaller stadiums in Denmark, it has been decided to continue playing all of the qualifying matches for the European Championships and World Cup only at Parken Stadium.<ref>{{cite web|title=Ny aftale mellem DBU og Parken|url=http://www.dr.dk/Sporten/Fodbold/Landsholdet/2009/11/17/142444.htm|author=DR Sporten|date=17 November 2009|language=da}}</ref> Those friendly matches, which are believed to create a high interest from spectators, will also continue to be played at Parken. For example, the friendly against Germany in August 2010 was played at Parken Stadium due to the high expectation of spectators desiring to watch the match. However, after Denmark's performance at the 2010 World Cup, the friendly only drew 19,071 spectators.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landsholdsdatabasen kamp information: Danmark-Tyskland (2–2)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseMatchInfo.aspx?matchid=5976|author=DBU|date=11 August 2010|language=da|access-date=25 October 2010|archive-date=18 May 2011|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518141214/http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/Landsholdsdatabasen/LBasematchInfo.aspx?matchid=5976|url-status=dead}}</ref> From September 1992 to January 2012, Denmark played a total of 77 A-level matches at Parken Stadium, of which 49 were won, 16 were drawn and 12 lost.<ref>{{cite web|title=Landskampsstatistik -Parken (København)|url=http://www.dbu.dk/landshold/landsholdsdatabasen/LBaseCountryInfo.aspx?stadiumid=362&teamtypeid=330&genderid=1#match|author=DBU|language=da}}</ref>

== Results and fixtures ==
{{See also|Denmark national football team results (2020–present)}}
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

{{legend2|#CCFFCC|Win|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFFFCC|Draw|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFCCCC|Loss|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}
{{legend2|#FFFFFF|Fixture|border=1px solid #AAAAAA}}

===2025===

|score             = 0–0

|goals2            =

|location          = [[Copenhagen]], Denmark
|attendance        = 35,369
|referee           = [[Daniel Siebert (referee)|Daniel Siebert]] ([[German Football Association|Germany]])
|result            = D
}}
{{football box collapsible|format=1

|time              = 21:45 [[Eastern European Summer Time|EEST]]
|round             = [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group C|2026 World Cup qualification]]
|team1             = {{fb-rt|GRE}}
|score             = 0–3
|report            = https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/match/2044246/
|team2             = {{fb|DEN}}
|goals1            =
|goals2            =
*[[Mikkel Damsgaard|Damsgaard]] {{goal|32}}
*[[Andreas Christensen|Christensen]] {{goal|62}}
*[[Rasmus Højlund|Højlund]] {{goal|81}}
|stadium           = [[Karaiskakis Stadium]]
|location          = [[Piraeus]], Greece
|attendance        = 31,172
|referee           = Donatas Rumšas ([[Lithuanian Football Federation|Lithuania]])
|result            = W
}}
{{football box collapsible|format = 1
|date              = 9 October 2025
|time              = 20:45 [[Central European Summer Time|CEST]]
|round             = [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group C|2026 World Cup qualification]]
|team1             = {{fb-rt|BLR}}
|score             = 0–6
|report            = https://www.uefa.com/european-qualifiers/match/2044260/
|team2             = {{fb|DEN}}
|goals1            =
|goals2            =
*[[Victor Froholdt|Froholdt]] {{goal|14}}
*[[Rasmus Højlund|Højlund]] {{goal|19||45}}
*[[Patrick Dorgu|Dorgu]] {{goal|45+6}}
*[[Anders Dreyer|Dreyer]] {{goal|66||78}}
|stadium           = [[ZTE Arena]]
|location          = [[Zalaegerszeg]], Hungary
|attendance        = 0
|referee           = Filip Glova ([[Slovak Football Association|Slovakia]])
|result            = W
|note              = Due to the [[Belarusian involvement in the Russian invasion of Ukraine|Belarusian involvement]] in the [[Russian invasion of Ukraine]], Belarus are required to play their home matches at neutral venues and [[Behind closed doors (sport)|behind closed doors]].<ref name="Belarusian involvement"/>
}}

==Coaching staff==

|+Coaching staff
!Position
!Name
|-

|-
|{{flagicon|DEN}} Kenneth Weber
